A vector database stores embeddings so an application can search by similarity instead of exact string matching. In a RAG project, each chunk of a document becomes a vector plus some payload metadata. The payload usually includes the document identifier, the original filename, the chunk index, and the chunk text itself. When a user submits a question, the application embeds the query using the same embedding model and asks the vector database for the nearest neighbors. The result is a ranked list of chunks that are close to the query in vector space.

Qdrant is a common choice for local development because it is easy to run and has a clear Python client. A typical setup defines one shared collection for all document chunks. That collection needs a vector size that matches the embedding model. For example, the model sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2 produces 384 dimensional embeddings, so the collection should be created with size 384 and a cosine distance metric. Cosine similarity is a natural fit when embeddings are normalized, because it compares direction in vector space rather than raw magnitude. That makes the ranking more stable for semantic search.

Each stored point should have a unique point identifier, but that point identifier is not the same thing as the document identifier. One document can create many chunk vectors, so a common strategy is to build point IDs like document-id colon chunk-index. The payload still stores the original document_id so later filtering remains easy. This distinction becomes important when a search endpoint grows from document scoped retrieval into corpus wide retrieval. Indexing is still done per document, but semantic search usually runs across the whole collection.

Payload metadata is valuable beyond debugging. It allows the retrieval layer to return human friendly information such as the source filename and the chunk index. It also enables future filtering. A system might later support searching only one document, only one customer, or only files uploaded within a date range. Even in a simple prototype, storing good payload fields early prevents painful migrations later. The vector database is not just a pile of float arrays. It is part of the document retrieval model, and payload design is part of the application contract.

When testing a vector database integration, useful questions include what metadata is stored with each vector chunk, why cosine similarity is used, and how Qdrant handles semantic search. Those questions should return chunks that mention payload metadata, nearest neighbor search, vector size, and cosine distance. If your corpus also contains unrelated backend material such as queue workers or retry policies, the semantic search results should still favor the vector database document for those vector specific questions.
